name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
۞ قُلْ تَعَالَوْا۟ أَتْلُ مَا حَرَّمَ رَبُّكُمْ عَلَيْكُمْ ۖ أَلَّا تُشْرِكُوا۟ بِهِۦ شَيْـًٔا ۖ وَبِٱلْوَٰلِدَيْنِ إِحْسَـٰنًا ۖ وَلَا تَقْتُلُوٓا۟ أَوْلَـٰدَكُم مِّنْ إِمْلَـٰقٍ ۖ نَّحْنُ نَرْزُقُكُمْ وَإِيَّاهُمْ ۖ وَلَا تَقْرَبُوا۟ ٱلْفَوَٰحِشَ مَا ظَهَرَ مِنْهَا وَمَا بَطَنَ ۖ وَلَا تَقْتُلُوا۟ ٱلنَّفْسَ ٱلَّتِى حَرَّمَ ٱللَّهُ إِلَّا بِٱلْحَقِّ ۚ ذَٰلِكُمْ وَصَّىٰكُم بِهِۦ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ (١٥١)
```

```english
Say, ‘Come, I will recount what your Lord has forbidden you. That you shall not ascribe any partners to Him, and you shall be good to the parents, you shall not kill your children due to penury—We will provide for you and for them—you shall not approach indecencies, the outward among them and the inward ones, and you shall not kill a soul [whose life] Allah has made inviolable, except with due cause. This is what He has enjoined upon you so that you may exercise your reason. (151)
```

```arabic
وَلَا تَقْرَبُوا۟ مَالَ ٱلْيَتِيمِ إِلَّا بِٱلَّتِى هِىَ أَحْسَنُ حَتَّىٰ يَبْلُغَ أَشُدَّهُۥ ۖ وَأَوْفُوا۟ ٱلْكَيْلَ وَٱلْمِيزَانَ بِٱلْقِسْطِ ۖ لَا نُكَلِّفُ نَفْسًا إِلَّا وُسْعَهَا ۖ وَإِذَا قُلْتُمْ فَٱعْدِلُوا۟ وَلَوْ كَانَ ذَا قُرْبَىٰ ۖ وَبِعَهْدِ ٱللَّهِ أَوْفُوا۟ ۚ ذَٰلِكُمْ وَصَّىٰكُم بِهِۦ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ (١٥٢)
```

```english
Do not approach the orphan’s property, except in the best [possible] manner, until he comes of age. And observe fully the measure and the balance with justice.’ We task no soul except according to its capacity. ‘And when you speak, be fair, even if it were a relative; and fulfill Allah’s covenant. This is what He enjoins upon you so that you may take admonition.’ (152)
```

```arabic
وَأَنَّ هَـٰذَا صِرَٰطِى مُسْتَقِيمًا فَٱتَّبِعُوهُ ۖ وَلَا تَتَّبِعُوا۟ ٱلسُّبُلَ فَتَفَرَّقَ بِكُمْ عَن سَبِيلِهِۦ ۚ ذَٰلِكُمْ وَصَّىٰكُم بِهِۦ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَّقُونَ (١٥٣)
```

```english
‘This indeed is my straight path, so follow it, and do not follow [other] ways, for they will separate you from His way. This is what He enjoins upon you so that you may be Godwary.’ (153)
```

```arabic
ثُمَّ ءَاتَيْنَا مُوسَى ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ تَمَامًا عَلَى ٱلَّذِىٓ أَحْسَنَ وَتَفْصِيلًا لِّكُلِّ شَىْءٍ وَهُدًى وَرَحْمَةً لَّعَلَّهُم بِلِقَآءِ رَبِّهِمْ يُؤْمِنُونَ (١٥٤)
```

```english
Then We gave Moses the Book, completing [Our blessing] on him who is virtuous, and as an elaboration of all things, and as a guidance and mercy, so that they may believe in the encounter with their Lord. (154)
```

```arabic
وَهَـٰذَا كِتَـٰبٌ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ مُبَارَكٌ فَٱتَّبِعُوهُ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ (١٥٥)
```

```english
And this Book that We have sent down is a blessed one; so follow it, and be Godwary so that you may receive [His] mercy. (155)
```

```arabic
أَن تَقُولُوٓا۟ إِنَّمَآ أُنزِلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبُ عَلَىٰ طَآئِفَتَيْنِ مِن قَبْلِنَا وَإِن كُنَّا عَن دِرَاسَتِهِمْ لَغَـٰفِلِينَ (١٥٦)
```

```english
Lest you should say, ‘The Book was sent down only to two communities before us, and we were indeed unaware of their studies,’ (156)
```

```arabic
أَوْ تَقُولُوا۟ لَوْ أَنَّآ أُنزِلَ عَلَيْنَا ٱلْكِتَـٰبُ لَكُنَّآ أَهْدَىٰ مِنْهُمْ ۚ فَقَدْ جَآءَكُم بَيِّنَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ وَهُدًى وَرَحْمَةٌ ۚ فَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّن كَذَّبَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ وَصَدَفَ عَنْهَا ۗ سَنَجْزِى ٱلَّذِينَ يَصْدِفُونَ عَنْ ءَايَـٰتِنَا سُوٓءَ ٱلْعَذَابِ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَصْدِفُونَ (١٥٧)
```

```english
or [lest] you should say, ‘If the Book had been sent down to us, surely we would have been better-guided than them.’ There has already come to you a manifest proof from your Lord and a guidance and mercy. So who is a greater wrongdoer than him who denies the signs of Allah, and turns away from them? Soon We shall requite those who turn away from Our signs with a terrible punishment because of what they used to evade. (157)
```

## (Commentary)

The verses clarify the general prohibitions (المحرمات العامة) that are not exclusive to any specific divine law (شريعة) among the divine laws (الشرائع الإلهية). These include: associating partners with Allah (الشرك بالله), failing to show excellence toward parents (ترك الإحسان بالوالدين), committing indecencies (الفواحش), and killing a soul that Allah has made inviolable (النفس المحترمة) without a just cause—which includes killing children for fear of poverty (إملاق)—approaching the property of the orphan except in the best manner, failing to give full measure and weight with justice (القسط), injustice in speech (الظلم في القول), failing to fulfill the covenant of Allah (عهد الله), and following paths other than the way of Allah (سبيل الله), which leads to division in religion.

Evidence that these are universal laws is found in what Allah, the Glorified, has narrated from the discourses of the Prophets to their nations in their religious proclamations, such as what has been narrated from Nuh, Hud, Salih, Ibrahim, Lut, Shu‘ayb, Musa, ‘Isa, and others (peace be upon them). The Exalted has said: **شَرَعَ لَكُمْ مِنَ الدِّينِ ما وَصَّى بِهِ نُوحاً وَالَّذِي أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ وَما وَصَّيْنا بِهِ إِبْراهِيمَ وَمُوسى وَعِيسى أَنْ أَقِيمُوا الدِّينَ وَلا تَتَفَرَّقُوا فِيهِ** — "He has prescribed for you the religion which He had enjoined upon Noah and which We have revealed to you, and which We had enjoined upon Abraham, Moses and Jesus, declaring, ‘Maintain the religion, and do not be divided regarding it.’" (42:13). Among the subtlest indications is the use of the term "enjoinment" or "bequest" (التوصية) regarding what was given to Nuh, Ibrahim, Musa, and ‘Isa (peace be upon them), and then the use of the same term "bequest" in these three verses that recount the foundations of divine prohibitions, where He says: **ذلِكُمْ وَصَّاكُمْ بِهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ** — "This is what He has enjoined upon you so that you may exercise your reason," **ذلِكُمْ وَصَّاكُمْ بِهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ** — "This is what He has enjoined upon you so that you may take admonition," and **ذلِكُمْ وَصَّاكُمْ بِهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَّقُونَ** — "This is what He has enjoined upon you so that you may be God-conscious."

Furthermore, reflection upon them reveals that the divine religion (الدين الإلهي) cannot be established nor can its state remain upright without any of these, no matter how concise or simple it may be, and no matter how simple the person professing it may be.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قُلْ تَعالَوْا أَتْلُ ما حَرَّمَ رَبُّكُمْ عَلَيْكُمْ أَلَّا تُشْرِكُوا بِهِ شَيْئاً** — "Say, ‘Come, I will recite what your Lord has forbidden you: that you shall not ascribe any partners to Him...’" It is said that **تعال** (Come) is derived from highness (العلو); it is a command given on the assumption that the commander is in a high place, even if that is not the case in reality. **التلاوة** (Recitation) is close in meaning to reading (القراءة). His saying **عَلَيْكُمْ** (upon you) is connected to His saying **أَتْلُ** (I will recite) or His saying **حَرَّمَ** (has forbidden) by way of grammatical competition (التنازع) over the prepositional phrase. It has also been suggested that **عَلَيْكُمْ** is a verbal noun meaning "take up" or "adhere to," and His saying **أَلَّا تُشْرِكُوا** (that you shall not associate) is its object, making the structure: "Adhere to not associating anything with Him and to excellence toward parents, etc." However, this contradicts what immediately comes to mind from the context (السياق).

Since His saying **تَعالَوْا أَتْلُ ما حَرَّمَ** (Come, I will recite what has been forbidden) is an invitation to recitation, the speech presents the very essence of what the revelation (الوحي) brought regarding the prohibitions—prohibiting some and commanding the opposite in others. Thus, He said: **أَلَّا تُشْرِكُوا بِهِ شَيْئاً** (that you shall not associate anything with Him), just as He said: **وَلا تَقْتُلُوا أَوْلادَكُمْ مِنْ إِمْلاقٍ** (and do not kill your children because of poverty) and **وَلا تَقْرَبُوا الْفَواحِشَ** (and do not approach indecencies), etc. And He said: **وَبِالْوالِدَيْنِ إِحْساناً** (and excellence toward parents), just as He said: **وَأَوْفُوا الْكَيْلَ وَالْمِيزانَ** (and give full measure and weight) and **وَإِذا قُلْتُمْ فَاعْدِلُوا** (and when you speak, be just), etc.

Polytheism (الشرك) was mentioned before all other prohibitions because it is the "great injustice" (الظلم العظيم) for which there is no hope of divine forgiveness. He said: **إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَغْفِرُ أَنْ يُشْرَكَ بِهِ وَيَغْفِرُ ما دُونَ ذلِكَ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ** — "Indeed Allah does not forgive that any partner should be ascribed to Him, but He forgives anything besides that to whomever He wishes." (4:48). Every sin ultimately traces back to it, just as every good deed ultimately traces back to monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — in some way.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَبِالْوالِدَيْنِ إِحْساناً** — "and excellence toward parents." That is: show excellence (أحسنوا) toward parents with true excellence (إحسانا). In *al-Majma‘*, it is said: "That is, He enjoined (أوصى) excellence toward parents." This is supported by the fact that "He forbade such-and-such" (حرم كذا) carries the meaning of enjoining its prohibition and commanding its avoidance. End quote.

In several places in the Noble Quran, excellence toward parents is mentioned immediately after monotheism and the negation of polytheism. Thus, it is commanded after the command for monotheism or the prohibition of polytheism, such as His saying: **وَقَضى رَبُّكَ أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوا إِلَّا إِيَّاهُ وَبِالْوالِدَيْنِ إِحْساناً** — "Your Lord has decided that you shall not worship anyone but Him, and [He has enjoined] kindness to parents." (17:23), and His saying: **وَإِذْ قالَ لُقْمانُ لِابْنِهِ وَهُوَ يَعِظُهُ يا بُنَيَّ لا تُشْرِكْ بِاللهِ إِنَّ الشِّرْكَ لَظُلْمٌ عَظِيمٌ وَوصَّيْنَا الْإِنْسانَ بِوالِدَيْهِ** — "When Luqman said to his son, as he advised him: ‘O my son! Do not ascribe any partners to Allah. Polytheism is indeed a great injustice.’ We have enjoined man concerning his parents..." (31:13-14), among other verses.

This indicates that being undutiful to parents (عقوق الوالدين) is among the greatest sins, or rather the greatest after polytheism against Allah, the Great. Rational consideration (الاعتبار) leads to this conclusion as well; for human society—without which neither life nor religion can be sustained for man—is a conventional, social construct (أمر وضعي اعتباري) that is preserved in its origin and survival only by the love of offspring. This love relies on the bond of mercy (رابطة الرحمة) formed within the home, maintained by the parents on one side and the children on the other. Children only need the parents' mercy and excellence at a time when the parents' souls naturally yearn for children. This natural inclination is sufficient as a motive and incentive for the parents to show excellence toward them. This is in contrast to the parents' need for the children's kindness and mercy, which naturally coincides with the parents' old age and the day of their inability to independently manage the necessities of their lives, while the children are in their youth and strength, focused on their own affairs.

The children's harshness toward parents and their undutifulness to them on the day of their need and hope, if widespread among the species, leads in turn to the nullification of the instinct for procreation and upbringing. This, from one perspective, leads to the abandonment of reproduction and the cessation of the lineage, and from another, to a distaste for establishing a home, lethargy in forming the "small society" (the family), and a refusal to maintain the mark (سمة) of fatherhood and motherhood. This results in the formation of a generation of human offspring with no kinship (قرابة) between them and no trace of the bond of the womb (رابطة الرحم) among them. At that point, the components of society disintegrate, its unity is shattered, its gathering is dispersed, and its affairs are corrupted with a corruption that no current law or established custom can rectify. The happiness of this world and the Hereafter departs from them. We shall provide an extensive discussion on this religious reality, Allah willing.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلا تَقْتُلُوا أَوْلادَكُمْ مِنْ إِمْلاقٍ نَحْنُ نَرْزُقُكُمْ وَإِيَّاهُمْ** — "and do not kill your children because of poverty; We provide for you and for them." **الإملاق** (Poverty) means insolvency regarding wealth and provisions; from it comes the word *al-tamalluq* (fawning/flattery). This was like an established custom among the Arabs in the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية) due to the rapid onset of drought and famine in their lands. A man, when threatened by insolvency, would hasten to kill his children out of pride, loathing to see them in the humiliation of destitution and hunger.

The prohibition was justified by His saying: **نَحْنُ نَرْزُقُكُمْ وَإِيَّاهُمْ** (We provide for you and for them). That is: you only kill them for fear that you will not be able to provide for them, yet you are not their providers; rather, Allah provides for you and for them all, so do not kill them.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلا تَقْرَبُوا الْفَواحِشَ ما ظَهَرَ مِنْها وَما بَطَنَ** — "and do not approach indecencies, the outward among them and the inward." **الفواحش** (Indecencies) is the plural of *fahishah*, which is a heinous and loathsome matter. Allah has counted among them in His speech adultery (الزنا), sodomy (اللواط), and slandering chaste women (قذف المحصنات). It is apparent that what is meant by "the outward and the inward" is the public and the secret, such as public adultery and taking secret paramours and mistresses.

In the legitimization of indecency lies the nullification of its heinousness and repulsiveness. This leads to its prevalence, for it is among the greatest things the soul yearns for—the soul that hates to be struck with deprivation from its most intense pleasures and to be veiled from the most amazing things to which its desire is attached and determined. In its prevalence lies the cessation of lineage and the destruction of the domestic society, and in its destruction lies the destruction of the large human society. We shall complete this discussion, Allah willing, in its appropriate place.

Similarly, the legitimization of killing and the indecencies that follow it is a nullification of public security, and in its nullification is the collapse of the structure of human society and the scattering of its pillars.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلا تَقْتُلُوا النَّفْسَ الَّتِي حَرَّمَ اللهُ إِلَّا بِالْحَقِ** — "and do not kill a soul [whose life] Allah has made inviolable, except with due cause." That is, Allah has forbidden its killing or has made it inviolable (حرمها) with the legislated sanctity (الحرمة المشرعة) that guards and protects it from being wasted in blood or right. It is said that He, the Exalted, repeated the mention of killing—even though it is included in "indecencies"—to emphasize its gravity and magnify its matter. Similar to this is the mention of killing children for fear of poverty; it was singled out for mention due to special concern, as the Arabs used to do that under the claim that the fear of poverty permits a father to kill his children to preserve his dignity from degradation, and they considered fatherhood a form of ownership.

Allah the Exalted has excepted from the prohibition of killing an inviolable soul—which is the soul of a Muslim or a person under a covenant (المعاهد)—killing it "with due cause" (بالحق), which is killing by way of legal retribution (القود) or a legislated legal punishment (الحد الشرعي).

Then He emphasized the prohibition of the aforementioned matters in the verse by saying: **ذلِكُمْ وَصَّاكُمْ بِهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ** — "This is what He has enjoined upon you so that you may exercise your reason." The reason for justifying these five prohibitions with the phrase "so that you may exercise your reason" will be explained shortly.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلا تَقْرَبُوا مالَ الْيَتِيمِ إِلَّا بِالَّتِي هي أَحْسَنُ حَتَّى يَبْلُغَ أَشُدَّهُ** — "And do not approach the orphan’s property except in the best manner, until he comes of age." The prohibition of "approaching" (القرب) is to indicate comprehensiveness; thus, it is not permissible to consume his wealth, use it, or perform any transaction (تصرف) with it except in the way that is the best of all conceivable ways for its preservation. This prohibition extends and the sanctity continues until he "comes of age" (يبلغ أشده). Once he comes of age, he is no longer an orphan incapable of managing his wealth, and he becomes the one to dispose of his own property without a natural need for a guardian's (الولي) management.

From here it becomes clear that what is meant by "coming of age" (بلوغه أشده) is physical maturity (البلوغ) and intellectual maturity (الرشد), as also indicated by His saying: **وَابْتَلُوا الْيَتامى حَتَّى إِذا بَلَغُوا النِّكاحَ فَإِنْ آنَسْتُمْ مِنْهُمْ رُشْداً فَادْفَعُوا إِلَيْهِمْ أَمْوالَهُمْ وَلا تَأْكُلُوها إِسْرافاً وَبِداراً أَنْ يَكْبَرُوا** — "Test the orphans until they reach the age of marriage. Then if you discern in them maturity, deliver to them their property. And do not consume it wastefully and in haste lest they should grow up." (4:6).

It also becomes clear that the purpose of limiting the prohibition of disposing of the orphan's wealth with the phrase "until he comes of age" is not to lift the prohibition after he comes of age and permit [the guardian's] disposal then; rather, the purpose is to clarify the time when it is appropriate to approach his wealth [for his benefit] and the cessation of the subject (ارتفاع الموضوع) thereafter. For the speech carries the meaning: "Improve the wealth of the orphan who is unable to improve and grow his own wealth until he grows up and becomes capable."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَأَوْفُوا الْكَيْلَ وَالْمِيزانَ بِالْقِسْطِ لا نُكَلِّفُ نَفْساً إِلَّا وُسْعَها** — "and give full measure and weight with justice. We do not task any soul except according to its capacity." **الإيفاء بالقسط** (Giving full measure with justice) is to act with fairness in both without any deficiency (بخس). His saying **لا نُكَلِّفُ نَفْساً إِلَّا وُسْعَها** (We do not task any soul except according to its capacity) is in the position of repelling an objection (دفع الدخل), as if it were said: "Giving full measure with justice and attaining true, objective fairness is not possible for the human soul, which has no choice in such matters but to rely on approximation." So the answer was: "We do not task any soul except according to its capacity." It is possible that His saying **لا نُكَلِّفُ نَفْساً إِلَّا وُسْعَها** relates to both rulings, namely: **وَلا تَقْرَبُوا مالَ الْيَتِيمِ** (And do not approach the orphan’s property...) and **وَأَوْفُوا الْكَيْلَ وَالْمِيزانَ** (and give full measure and weight...).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِذا قُلْتُمْ فَاعْدِلُوا وَلَوْ كانَ ذا قُرْبى** — "And when you speak, be just, even if [it should be] a relative." The mention of the relative (ذا القربى)—the one whom the emotion of kinship and the womb (الرحم) invites one to protect and shield from evil or harm to his person or wealth—indicates that what is meant by "speech" (القول) is the speech upon which the benefit or harm of another may depend. The mention of "justice" (العدل) in speech supports this, indicating that there is a potential for injustice (ظلم) and that the speech relates to certain rights, such as testimony (الشهادة), adjudication (القضاء), issuing legal opinions (الفتوى), and the like.

The meaning is: "Be mindful of your words that contain benefit or harm for people and be just in them. Let not mercy, kindness, or any emotion drive you to favor one side, thereby distorting speech and overstepping the truth to testify or judge in a way that favors the one you love and nullifies the right of the one you dislike."

It is said in *al-Majma‘*: "This is among the eloquent commands which, despite the fewness of its letters, encompasses confessions (الأقارير), testimonies, bequests (الوصايا), legal opinions, cases, rulings, doctrines (المذاهب), and commanding the right and forbidding the wrong."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَبِعَهْدِ اللهِ أَوْفُوا** — "and fulfill the covenant of Allah." Al-Raghib said in *al-Mufradat*: "**العهد** (The covenant) is the preservation of a thing and its observance state after state." End quote. Therefore, it is applied to divine decrees (الفرامين), legislated obligations (التكاليف المشرعة), assigned duties (الوظائف), as well as the covenant that is a solemn pledge (الموثق), the vow (النذر), and the oath (اليمين).

The frequent use of it in the Noble Quran for divine decrees, its attribution in the verse to Allah the Glorified, and the suitability of the context—which contains the clarification of general divine rulings and bequests—all support that what is meant by **وَبِعَهْدِ اللهِ أَوْفُوا** is the divine religious obligations (التكاليف الدينية الإلهية). This is so even if it is possible that the covenant refers to a pledge made by saying, for example, "I have covenanted with Allah to do such-and-such." The Exalted said: **وَأَوْفُوا بِالْعَهْدِ إِنَّ الْعَهْدَ كانَ مَسْؤُلاً** — "and fulfill the covenant; indeed the covenant is accountable." (17:34). In that case, its attribution to Allah would be like the attribution of testimony to Him in His saying: **وَلا نَكْتُمُ شَهادَةَ اللهِ** — "and we do not conceal the testimony of Allah" (5:106), to indicate that the transaction therein is with Him, the Glorified. Then He emphasized the obligations mentioned in the verse by saying: **ذلِكُمْ وَصَّاكُمْ بِهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ** — "This is what He has enjoined upon you so that you may take admonition."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَأَنَّ هذا صِراطِي مُسْتَقِيماً فَاتَّبِعُوهُ وَلا تَتَّبِعُوا السُّبُلَ فَتَفَرَّقَ بِكُمْ عَنْ سَبِيلِهِ** — "‘This is My straight path, so follow it, and do not follow [other] ways, for they will separate you from His way.’" to the end of the verse. It has been recited as **وَأَنَّ** with the *fathah* on the *hamzah* and the doubling of the *nun* (or its lightening), as if it is conjoined (بالعطف) to the position of His saying: **أَلَّا تُشْرِكُوا بِهِ شَيْئاً**. It has also been recited with the *kasrah* on the *hamzah* as a new sentence (الاستئناف).

What the context of the verses provides is that the content of this verse is one of the bequests that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was commanded to recite to them and inform them of, as it was said: **قُلْ تَعالَوْا أَتْلُ ما حَرَّمَ رَبُّكُمْ عَلَيْكُمْ**. This necessitates that His saying **وَأَنَّ هذا صِراطِي مُسْتَقِيماً فَاتَّبِعُوهُ** is presented not because the primary purpose lies in it itself—since the universals of religion were completed in the two verses preceding it—but rather to serve as a preamble and preparation (توطئة وتمهيد) for His saying after it: **وَلا تَتَّبِعُوا السُّبُلَ**. Similarly, this very sentence is like a preamble for His saying: **فَتَفَرَّقَ بِكُمْ عَنْ سَبِيلِهِ**. Thus, the meaning of the verse is: "Do not separate from His way and do not differ regarding it." The verse is thus presented in the same manner as His saying: **شَرَعَ لَكُمْ مِنَ الدِّينِ ما وَصَّى بِهِ نُوحاً وَالَّذِي أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ وَما وَصَّيْنا بِهِ إِبْراهِيمَ وَمُوسى وَعِيسى أَنْ أَقِيمُوا الدِّينَ وَلا تَتَفَرَّقُوا فِيهِ** — "He has prescribed for you the religion which He had enjoined upon Noah and which We have revealed to you, and which We had enjoined upon Abraham, Moses and Jesus, declaring, ‘Maintain the religion, and do not be divided regarding it.’" (42:13). The command in the verse to maintain the religion is what was enjoined of the prescribed religion, as if it were repeated to serve as a preparation for the prohibition of division in religion.

The meaning is: "Among what your Lord has forbidden you and enjoined upon you is that you do not follow the ways other than this straight path (الصراط المستقيم) — *the direct and unerring way to Allah* — which accepts neither deviation nor difference. These are ways other than the way of Allah, for following ways other than it separates you from His way, so you differ regarding it and exit the straight path, since the straight path has no difference between its parts nor between those who traverse it."

The requirement of the apparent context is that what is meant by **صِراطِي** (My path) is the path of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), for he is the one addressing the people with these obligations by a command from his Lord when He says: **قُلْ تَعالَوْا أَتْلُ** (Say, ‘Come, I will recite...’). He is the one speaking to them and addressing them, while Allah the Glorified holds the position of the third person (الغيبة) in the verses, even at the end of this verse where He says: **فَتَفَرَّقَ بِكُمْ عَنْ سَبِيلِهِ ذلِكُمْ وَصَّاكُمْ بِهِ** (for they will separate you from His way. This is what He has enjoined upon you). There is no harm in attributing the straight path to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), for the straight path has been attributed to a group of His servants upon whom Allah has bestowed favors—the prophets, the truthful, the martyrs, and the righteous—in His saying: **اهْدِنَا الصِّراطَ الْمُسْتَقِيمَ ، صِراطَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ** — "Guide us on the straight path, the path of those whom You have blessed" (1:6-7).

However, the commentators seem to have accepted that the first-person pronoun in His saying **صِراطِي** refers to Allah the Glorified. Thus, there is a type of shift in address (الالتفات) in the verse, but not in His saying **صِراطِي**, rather in His saying **عَنْ سَبِيلِهِ** (from His way). For the meaning of the verse would be: "Come, I will recite to you what your Lord has enjoined upon you, which is that He says to you: ‘Indeed, this is My straight path, so follow it,’" or His bequest is: "‘Indeed, this is My straight path, so follow it, and do not follow [other] ways, for they will separate you from My way.’" So the shift—as mentioned—is only in His saying **عَنْ سَبِيلِهِ**.

In any case, He, the Exalted, in the verse names what He mentioned of the universals of religion as His straight path, which does not fail in guiding its travelers and bringing them to the destination, and has no difference between its parts nor between its travelers as long as they remain upon it, so they never separate. Then He forbids them from following other ways, for it is the nature of those ways to cast difference and division, because they are the paths of satanic whims (الأهواء الشيطانية) which have no standard to regulate them, unlike the way of Allah which is built upon the primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — and the creation, and there is no change in the creation of Allah; that is the upright religion. Then He, the Glorified, emphasized His ruling in the verse by saying: **ذلِكُمْ وَصَّاكُمْ بِهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَّقُونَ** — "This is what He has enjoined upon you so that you may be God-conscious."

The endings (الخواتيم) differed in the three verses: the first verse ended with His saying **ذلِكُمْ وَصَّاكُمْ بِهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ** (so that you may exercise your reason), the second with His saying **ذلِكُمْ وَصَّاكُمْ بِهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ** (so that you may take admonition), and the third with His saying **ذلِكُمْ وَصَّاكُمْ بِهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَّقُونَ** (so that you may be God-conscious).

Perhaps the reason for this is that the matters mentioned in the first verse—polytheism against Allah the Great, undutifulness to parents, killing children due to poverty, and approaching heinous indecencies and killing an inviolable soul without right—are things whose prohibition the human primordial nature perceives at first glance. No human, who is distinguished from other animals by reason (العقل), dares to commit them unless he follows whims and is surrounded by dark emotions that cast a thick veil before the reason. Thus, merely clinging to the protection of reason in general and exiting from pure whims reveals to man their prohibition and their ill-effect upon man as a human being. Therefore, it ended with His saying: **ذلِكُمْ وَصَّاكُمْ بِهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ**.

As for what was mentioned in the second verse—avoiding the orphan's wealth, giving full measure and weight with justice, justice in speech, and fulfilling the covenant of Allah—these are matters not at the same level of obviousness as those recited in the first verse. Rather, man, along with being equipped with reason, needs to "take admonition" (التذكر) — *the act of recalling and reflecting upon truths* — which is to return to the general benefits and harms known to the primordial reason, so that he may perceive the corruption within them that destroys the structure of his society and brings him and the rest of his species to destruction. For what good remains in a human society where the young and weak are not shown mercy, where measure and weight are cheated, where judgment and adjudication are not just, and where the word of truth is not heeded? For this subtle point, the verse ended with His saying: **ذلِكُمْ وَصَّاكُمْ بِهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ**.

The purpose for which the third verse was presented is the prohibition of division and difference in religion by following ways other than the way of Allah. Following those ways is such that religious godwariness (التقوى) — *the state of being God-conscious and guarding oneself against sin* — cannot be attained except by avoiding them.

This is because religious godwariness is only achieved by insight into the divine prohibitions and refraining from His forbidden matters through the exercise of reason and taking admonition; in other words, by adhering to the human primordial nature upon which the religion is built. The Exalted has said: **وَنَفْسٍ وَما سَوَّاها فَأَلْهَمَها فُجُورَها وَتَقْواها** — "by the soul and Him who fashioned it, and inspired it with [the discernment of] its wickedness and its God-consciousness" (91:7-8). Allah has promised the God-conscious that if they are God-conscious, He will aid them with that which clarifies their path and distinguishes between truth and falsehood for them, saying: **وَمَنْ يَتَّقِ اللهَ يَجْعَلْ لَهُ مَخْرَجاً** — "and whoever is God-conscious, He will make for him a way out" (65:2), and saying: **إِنْ تَتَّقُوا اللهَ يَجْعَلْ لَكُمْ فُرْقاناً** — "If you are God-conscious, He will provide you with a criterion" (8:29).

Thus, one is on the path of godwariness as long as he is adherent to the way of exercising reason and taking admonition, proceeding according to the course of the primordial nature. If he deviates to the outside of this path—which is nothing but following whims, clinging to the earth, and being deceived by the glitter of the life of this world—whims and emotions pull him toward indulgence and devotion to opposing sound reason and abandoning religious godwariness, without concern for the ill-fated end that threatens him, like a drunkard who knows neither what he does nor what is being done to him.

Psychological whims (الأهواء النفسانية) are diverse; there is no standard to regulate them nor a system to govern them in which their followers can unite. Therefore, you hardly see two people of whims adhering to one path or accompanying each other to one goal. Allah the Glorified has counted for them in His speech various ways, such as His saying: **وَلِتَسْتَبِينَ سَبِيلُ الْمُجْرِمِينَ** — "and so that the way of the guilty may become clear" (6:55), His saying: **وَلا تَتَّبِعْ سَبِيلَ الْمُفْسِدِينَ** — "and do not follow the way of the agents of corruption" (7:142), His saying: **وَلا تَتَّبِعانِّ سَبِيلَ الَّذِينَ لا يَعْلَمُونَ** — "and do not follow the way of those who do not know" (10:89), and His saying regarding the polytheists: **إِنْ يَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا الظَّنَّ وَما تَهْوَى الْأَنْفُسُ وَلَقَدْ جاءَهُمْ مِنْ رَبِّهِمُ الْهُدى** — "They follow nothing but conjectures and what their souls desire, while guidance has already come to them from their Lord" (53:23). If you trace the verses of guidance and misguidance, and following and obedience, you will find much regarding this meaning.

In short, religious godwariness is not achieved through division and difference, nor by entering any path that is laid out, nor by walking in any valley that appears to the traveler. Rather, it is achieved by adhering to the straight path in which there is no failure nor difference. That is what is hoped to lead to being clothed in the garment of godwariness. Therefore, Allah the Glorified followed His saying **وَلا تَتَّبِعُوا السُّبُلَ فَتَفَرَّقَ بِكُمْ عَنْ سَبِيلِهِ** with His saying: **ذلِكُمْ وَصَّاكُمْ بِهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَّقُونَ**.

It is said in *Ruh al-Ma‘ani*: "The first verse was ended with His saying, the Glorified: **لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ**, and this one—meaning the second—with His saying: **لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ**, because the people were persisting in polytheism, killing children, approaching adultery, and killing the inviolable soul without right, without loathing them or reasoning their foulness. So He forbade them so that they might reason their foulness, thus loathing them and abandoning them. As for preserving the wealth of orphans, giving full measure, justice in speech, and fulfilling the covenant, they used to do these things and take pride in being characterized by them. So Allah the Exalted commanded them with that so that they might take admonition should forgetfulness occur to them. This was said by al-Qutb al-Razi." End quote.

You are well aware that what he mentioned regarding their being characterized by preserving the wealth of orphans, giving full measure, and justice in speech does not agree with what history has recorded of the traits of the Arabs in the Age of Ignorance. Furthermore, the way he interpreted "taking admonition" (التذكر) is the meaning of "remembrance" (الذكر) rather than "taking admonition" in the usage of the Quran.

Then he said: "The Imam—meaning al-Razi—said in *al-Tafsir al-Kabir*: ‘The reason for ending each verse with what it was ended with is that the five obligations mentioned in the first verse are manifest and clear, so it was necessary to reason and understand them. The four obligations mentioned in this verse—meaning the second—are hidden and obscure matters that require much exertion (الاجتهاد) and reflection until one stands upon the point of moderation (الاعتدال), which is taking admonition.’" End quote.

What he mentioned of the reason is close in approach to what we have presented, except that the four matters mentioned in the second verse are things man attains with the slightest reflection; they are not of that hiddenness and obscurity he described. Therefore, he was forced to return "taking admonition" to standing upon the limit of moderation in them rather than their essence, thereby corrupting the meaning of the verse. For the requirement of the context is the return of the hope of taking admonition to the essence of what was enjoined therein. That which naturally needs to stand upon its limit of moderation are the first two matters, namely approaching the orphan's wealth and giving full measure and weight, and their matter was addressed by His saying: **لا نُكَلِّفُ نَفْساً إِلَّا وُسْعَها**. So understand that.

Then he said regarding the third verse: "Abu Hayyan said: ‘Since the straight path is the gatherer of the obligations, and Allah the Glorified commanded its following and forbade following other paths, He ended that with godwariness, which is guarding against the Fire; for whoever follows His path attains eternal salvation and gains everlasting happiness.’" End quote.

This is based on making the command to follow the straight path in the verse that to which the primary intention is attached. It has already been mentioned that the requirement of the context is its being a preamble for the prohibition of division by following other ways, and a preparation for His saying: **وَلا تَتَّبِعُوا السُّبُلَ فَتَفَرَّقَ بِكُمْ عَنْ سَبِيلِهِ**.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ثُمَّ آتَيْنا مُوسَى الْكِتابَ تَماماً عَلَى الَّذِي أَحْسَنَ** — "Then We gave Moses the Book, completing [the blessing] on him who was virtuous..." to the end of the verse. Since what He mentioned and enjoined of the universals of the laws are legislated obligations general to all the religion given to the prophets—and they are universal, concise matters—this made it appropriate to shift to clarifying that He, the Glorified, after legislating them for all in a concise manner, detailed them where their detailing was required: for Musa (peace be upon him) first in what He sent down to him of the Book, and for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) second in what He sent down to him of a blessed Book. So He said: **ثُمَّ آتَيْنا مُوسَى الْكِتابَ تَماماً عَلَى الَّذِي أَحْسَنَ وَتَفْصِيلاً لِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ** etc.

The meaning of the verse is: "After We legislated the concise religious laws, We gave Musa the Book as a completion (تماماً) by which the deficiency of those who did good among them—in terms of the concise law—is completed, and as a detailing (تفصيلاً) by which every thing of the branches of these concise laws is detailed, of what the Children of Israel need, and as a guidance and mercy so that they might believe in the encounter with their Lord." This is what is provided by the context of the verse, which is connected to the context of the three preceding verses.

Thus, His saying **ثُمَّ آتَيْنا مُوسَى الْكِتابَ** is a return to the previous context that was before His saying **قُلْ تَعالَوْا أَتْلُ ما حَرَّمَ رَبُّكُمْ عَلَيْكُمْ** etc., which is the speech of Allah to His Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in the first-person plural form. It has been conveyed by the delay understood from the word **ثُمَّ** (Then) that this Book was only sent down to be a completion and a detailing for the conciseness that was in those general, universal laws.

The commentators have explained His saying **ثُمَّ آتَيْنا مُوسَى الْكِتابَ** with strange views:
Among them: that there is an omission in the speech, and the estimation is: "Then say, O Muhammad: We gave Musa the Book."
Among them: that the estimation is: "Then I inform you that Musa was given the Book."
Among them: that the estimation is: "Then I recite to you: We gave Musa the Book."
Among them: that it is connected to His saying in the story of Ibrahim: **وَوَهَبْنا لَهُ إِسْحاقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ** (And We gave him Isaac and Jacob), and the structure is: "And We gave him Isaac and Jacob, then We gave Musa the Book."

What drove them to these forced interpretations (التكلفات) is that the Torah was before the Quran, and the word **ثُمَّ** necessitates a delay (التراخي), which would imply the descent of the Torah after the Quran, while it was said before that: **قُلْ تَعالَوْا أَتْلُ ما حَرَّمَ رَبُّكُمْ عَلَيْكُمْ**. The previous explanation suffices you from the burden of these views.

His saying **تَماماً عَلَى الَّذِي أَحْسَنَ** (completing [the blessing] on him who was virtuous) clarifies that the sending down of the Book was so that the deficiency of those who did good among the Children of Israel in acting upon these universal, general laws might be completed. The Exalted said in the story of Musa after the descent of the Book: **وَكَتَبْنا لَهُ فِي الْأَلْواحِ مِنْ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ مَوْعِظَةً وَتَفْصِيلاً لِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ فَخُذْها بِقُوَّةٍ وَأْمُرْ قَوْمَكَ يَأْخُذُوا بِأَحْسَنِها** — "We wrote for him in the Tablets advice concerning all things and an elaboration of all things: ‘Hold on to them with power, and bid your people to hold on to the best of them.’" (7:145), and He said: **وَادْخُلُوا الْبابَ سُجَّداً وَقُولُوا حِطَّةٌ نَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ خَطاياكُمْ وَسَنَزِيدُ الْمُحْسِنِينَ** — "and enter the gate prostrating, and say, ‘Relief!’ We will forgive you your sins and We will soon enhance the virtuous." (2:58). Accordingly, the relative pronoun (الموصول) in His saying **عَلَى الَّذِي أَحْسَنَ** denotes the genus.

They have mentioned other views regarding the meaning of the sentence. It was said: "The meaning is: a completion upon the excellence of Musa with prophethood and honor." It was said: "The meaning is: a completion of the blessing upon those who did good among the believers." It was said: "The meaning is: a completion of the blessing upon the prophets who did good." It was said: "The meaning is: a completion of his honor in Paradise for his excellence in the world." It was said: "The meaning is: a completion upon that which Allah bestowed of excellence upon Musa of honor with prophethood and other things." It was said: "It is connected to the story of Ibrahim, and the meaning is: a completion of the blessing upon Ibrahim." The weakness of all these is apparent.

His saying **وَتَفْصِيلاً لِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ** (and an elaboration of all things) means of what the Children of Israel need or what others after them benefit from, and a guidance by which they are guided and a mercy by which they are blessed. His saying **لَعَلَّهُمْ بِلِقاءِ رَبِّهِمْ يُؤْمِنُونَ** (so that they may believe in the encounter with their Lord) contains an indication that the Children of Israel were sluggish or refused to believe in the encounter with Allah and the Last Day. This is supported by the fact that the current Torah—which the Quran mentions is distorted—contains no mention of the resurrection on the Day of Judgment. Some of their historians have mentioned that the people of Israel did not believe in the Return (المعاد).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَهذا كِتابٌ أَنْزَلْناهُ مُبارَكٌ** — "This is a blessed Book that We have sent down..." to the end of the verse. That is: this is a blessed Book that shares with the Book of Musa in what We mentioned of the characteristic, so follow it, etc.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَنْ تَقُولُوا إِنَّما أُنْزِلَ الْكِتابُ عَلى طائِفَتَيْنِ مِنْ قَبْلِنا** — "lest you should say, ‘The Book was only sent down to two groups before us...’" etc. **أَنْ تَقُولُوا** means "for fear that you say" or "lest you say," and this is common in speech. It is connected to His saying in the previous verse: **أَنْزَلْناهُ** (We have sent it down).

His saying **طائِفَتَيْنِ مِنْ قَبْلِنا** (two groups before us) refers to the Jews and the Christians, upon whom the Torah and the Gospel were sent down. As for the books of the prophets sent down before them which the Quran mentions, such as the Book of Nuh and the Book of Ibrahim (peace be upon them), they did not contain the detailing of the laws, even if they included their foundation. As for the rest of what is attributed to the prophets (peace be upon them) of books, such as the Psalms (زبور) of Dawud (peace be upon him) and others, they did not contain laws nor did they have a covenant regarding them.

The meaning is: "We sent down the Quran for fear that you say: ‘The divine Book detailing its laws was only sent down to two groups before us, the Jews and the Christians, and we were unaware of their study and recitation, and there is no blame upon us despite the unawareness.’"

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَوْ تَقُولُوا لَوْ أَنَّا أُنْزِلَ عَلَيْنَا الْكِتابُ لَكُنَّا أَهْدى مِنْهُمْ** — "or lest you should say, ‘If the Book had been sent down to us, we would surely have been better guided than them.’" to the end of the verse. That is, better guided than those to whom the Book was sent down before us. His saying **فَقَدْ جاءَكُمْ بَيِّنَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ** (Now there has come to you a clear proof from your Lord) is a derivation (تفريع) from both His sayings **أَنْ تَقُولُوا** and **أَوْ تَقُولُوا**. He substituted "the Book" with "clear proof" (بينة) to indicate the manifestness of its argument and the clarity of its indication, such that no excuse remains for an apologizer nor any pretext for a pretender. **الصدف** (Turning away) means aversion, and the meaning of the verse is clear.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *Tafsir al-‘Ayyashi*, narrated from Abu Basir, who said: "I was sitting with Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him)—and he was leaning on his cushion—when he recited the decisive verses (الآيات المحكمات) which nothing has abrogated from *al-An‘am*. He said: ‘Seventy thousand angels escorted them: **قُلْ تَعالَوْا أَتْلُ ما حَرَّمَ رَبُّكُمْ عَلَيْكُمْ ـ أَلَّا تُشْرِكُوا بِهِ شَيْئاً**.’"

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, ‘Abd ibn Humayd, Ibn Abi Hatim, Abu al-Shaykh, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Hakim (who authenticated it) narrated from ‘Ubadah ibn al-Samit, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: ‘Which of you will pledge allegiance to me upon these three verses?’ Then he recited: **قُلْ تَعالَوْا أَتْلُ ما حَرَّمَ رَبُّكُمْ عَلَيْكُمْ** to the end of the three verses."

Then he said: "So whoever fulfills them, his reward is with Allah. And whoever falls short in anything of them and Allah catches him in this world—that is his punishment. And whoever is delayed until the Hereafter, his matter is with Allah—if He wishes, He will punish him, and if He wishes, He will forgive him."

**I say:** The narration is not without issue, for among what is mentioned in the verses is polytheism against Allah, and the punishment of this world does not suffice for it, nor does it receive forgiveness in the Hereafter by the explicit text of the Quran. The Exalted said: **إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَغْفِرُ أَنْ يُشْرَكَ بِهِ** — "Indeed Allah does not forgive that any partner should be ascribed to Him" (4:48), and He said: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَماتُوا وَهُمْ كُفَّارٌ أُولئِكَ عَلَيْهِمْ لَعْنَةُ اللهِ وَالْمَلائِكَةِ وَالنَّاسِ أَجْمَعِينَ ، خالِدِينَ فِيها لا يُخَفَّفُ عَنْهُمُ الْعَذابُ وَلا هُمْ يُنْظَرُونَ** — "Indeed those who disbelieve and die while they are disbelievers—it is they on whom shall be the curse of Allah, the angels and all mankind. They will remain in it [forever]; their punishment shall not be lightened, nor shall they be granted any respite." (2:161-162).

Furthermore, the apparent sense of the narration is that these rulings are among what is exclusive to this law, as suggested by what was narrated from some of the Companions and the Successors, such as what was narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from a group from Ibn Mas‘ud, who said: "Whoever would be pleased to look at the bequest of Muhammad upon which is his seal—let him read these verses: **قُلْ تَعالَوْا أَتْلُ ما حَرَّمَ رَبُّكُمْ عَلَيْكُمْ** to His saying **لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَّقُونَ**." Similar to this is what was narrated from Mundhir al-Thawri from al-Rabi‘ ibn Khaytham.

In *Tafsir al-‘Ayyashi*, narrated from ‘Amr ibn Abi al-Miqdam from his father from ‘Ali ibn al-Husayn (peace be upon him): **الْفَواحِشَ ما ظَهَرَ مِنْها وَما بَطَنَ** (indecencies, the outward among them and the inward). He said: "What is outward is marrying the father's wife, and what is inward is adultery."

**I say:** This is by way of mentioning some of the instances (المصاديق).

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ahmad, ‘Abd ibn Humayd, al-Nasa’i, al-Bazzar, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Abu al-Shaykh, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Hakim (who authenticated it) narrated from Ibn Mas‘ud, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) drew a line with his hand and then said: ‘This is the way of Allah, straight.’ Then he drew lines to the right of that line and to its left—then he said: ‘And these are ways; there is not a way among them except that there is a devil upon it calling to it.’ Then he recited: **وَأَنَّ هذا صِراطِي مُسْتَقِيماً فَاتَّبِعُوهُ ـ وَلا تَتَّبِعُوا السُّبُلَ فَتَفَرَّقَ بِكُمْ عَنْ سَبِيلِهِ**."

In it, Ahmad, Ibn Majah, Ibn Abi Hatim, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Jabir ibn ‘Abdullah, who said: "We were sitting with the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and he drew a line like this in front of him—and said: ‘This is the way of Allah.’ And he drew two lines to its right and two lines to its left—and said: ‘This is the way of Satan.’ Then he placed his hand on the middle line and recited: **وَأَنَّ هذا صِراطِي مُسْتَقِيماً فَاتَّبِعُوهُ** the verse."

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*: al-Hasan ibn ‘Ali informed us from his father from al-Husayn ibn Sa‘id from Muhammad ibn Sinan from Abu Khalid al-Qammat from Abu Basir from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him): regarding His saying: **هذا صِراطِي مُسْتَقِيماً فَاتَّبِعُوهُ ـ وَلا تَتَّبِعُوا السُّبُلَ فَتَفَرَّقَ بِكُمْ عَنْ سَبِيلِهِ**. He said: "We are the way (السبيل); so whoever refuses, then these are the ways, and he has indeed disbelieved."

**I say:** This is of the nature of application (الجري). What he (peace be upon him) mentioned is derived from His saying, the Exalted: **قُلْ لا أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ أَجْراً إِلَّا الْمَوَدَّةَ فِي الْقُرْبى** — "Say, ‘I do not ask you any reward for it except love of [my] relatives.’" (42:23), when joined with His saying: **قُلْ ما أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ إِلَّا مَنْ شاءَ أَنْ يَتَّخِذَ إِلى رَبِّهِ سَبِيلاً** — "Say, ‘I do not ask you any reward for it, except that anyone who wishes should take the way to his Lord.’" (24:57).

Several narrations have been reported from both Shi‘i and Sunni chains that ‘Ali is the straight path, and reference to this has already been made in the commentary on Surah al-Fatihah in the first volume of the book.
